name: Disposition Effect
def: Investors sell winners too early and hold losers too long, a pattern Shefrin and Statman (1985) named and Odean (1998) confirmed in thousands of retail brokerage accounts. Realizing a gain feels like locking in success, while realizing a loss means admitting the original decision was wrong.
tell: You trim the stock that is up 40 percent to "take something off the table" while the position down 30 percent sits untouched, waiting to get back to even.
counter: Set exit rules at purchase and automate them before emotion gets a vote.

name: House Money Effect
def: After a windfall gain, people take risks they would normally refuse, treating recent profits as the casino's chips rather than their own capital. Thaler and Johnson (1990) showed prior gains loosen risk tolerance because a subsequent loss merely returns the gambler to break-even.
tell: A position doubles, so you roll the profit into a speculative small cap you never would have bought with your paycheck—the gain feels free, and you size it recklessly.
counter: Treat every dollar of gains as principal and size new positions accordingly.

name: Action Bias
def: Under uncertainty, doing something feels safer than doing nothing, even when inaction has the higher expected value. Goalkeepers dive on penalty kicks though staying centered stops more shots, and investors churn portfolios for the same reason: motion masquerades as control.
tell: Every red day tempts you to rebalance, hedge, or rotate sectors; the trade log fills up while the strategy the trades were meant to serve never changes.
counter: Before any trade, write down what changed in the thesis; if nothing did, close the ticket.

name: Status Quo Bias
def: Samuelson and Zeckhauser (1988) showed people disproportionately stick with the default option, whatever it happens to be. Because any change requires a decision that could later be regretted, the current allocation acquires an unearned presumption of correctness.
tell: Your 401(k) still sits in the target-date fund you picked on day one, and the legacy position you inherited five years ago has never faced a fresh buy-or-sell decision.
counter: Once a year, force every holding to re-earn its place from a zero-based portfolio.

name: Overconfidence
def: People systematically overrate the precision of their forecasts and the quality of their information; confidence grows faster than accuracy. Barber and Odean (2000) found that the retail investors who traded most, presumably the most confident, earned the lowest net returns.
tell: Your DCF prints a tight valuation range because you trust your revenue forecast to the decimal; sensitivity tables get built last, if at all, and the base case is really a best case.
counter: Widen every estimate until the downside scenario genuinely hurts, then decide.

name: Loss Aversion
def: In Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory, losses weigh roughly twice as heavily as equivalent gains, so the pain of losing $100 outweighs the pleasure of winning it. Decisions get made to avoid the feeling of loss rather than to maximize expected value.
tell: You refuse a sensible portfolio cleanup because selling would "lock in" losses, and you pass on asymmetric bets where the upside dwarfs a small, probable hit.
counter: Judge each position by future expected return, not by the pain of the exit.

name: Anchoring
def: Tversky and Kahneman (1974) showed that an arbitrary starting number pulls final estimates toward it, even when everyone knows the anchor is irrelevant. Adjustment away from the anchor is almost always insufficient, so the first figure on the table quietly frames the whole negotiation.
tell: Your purchase price becomes the reference for everything: a stock at $80 looks "cheap" only because you bought at $120, and your model's terminal multiple mysteriously matches management's guidance deck.
counter: Build your own estimate before looking at the price, the consensus, or the deck.

name: Confirmation Bias
def: People seek, weight, and remember evidence that supports what they already believe while discounting evidence that contradicts it. The search itself is biased: questions get framed so that almost any answer confirms, which is why a thesis rarely dies from its owner's own research.
tell: After buying, you read only bullish coverage, follow accounts that agree with you, and file the bear case under "noise"; the model gets updated only when new data flatters it.
counter: Write the strongest bear case yourself before adding to any position.

name: Recency Bias
def: The most recent observations dominate judgment, so people extrapolate the latest trend as if it were the permanent state of the world. Long-run base rates fade from view, which is why risk appetite peaks after rallies and vanishes after crashes, precisely backward from what valuations imply.
tell: Three green quarters and your model's growth rate quietly drifts toward the recent print; three red months and you hold cash at exactly the moment expected returns are highest.
counter: Start every forecast from ten-year base rates, not the latest quarter.

name: Hindsight Bias
def: Once an outcome is known, it feels as if it was predictable all along; Fischhoff (1975) showed that learning a result inflates how inevitable it seems in retrospect. People even misremember their own prior forecasts to match what happened, so the past compresses into a clean story that feeds overconfident prediction.
tell: Reviewing a losing trade, you "knew" the earnings miss was coming and conclude the process was fine—just execution; the journal entry from the day of purchase says otherwise.
counter: Record your reasoning and probabilities at decision time; grade only against that record.

name: Herding
def: People infer that a crowded trade must be an informed one, substituting the group's behavior for their own analysis. The comfort is real—being wrong with everyone is professionally safer than being wrong alone—but crowding pushes prices away from fundamentals and concentrates the exit.
tell: You buy the ticker everyone on the group chat already owns, sized larger because its popularity feels like diligence someone else must have done.
counter: State a reason to own it that would survive everyone else selling.

name: Mental Accounting
def: Thaler showed people sort money into separate mental buckets—salary, bonus, tax refund, "fun money"—and spend or risk each bucket by its label rather than treating wealth as fungible. The buckets change behavior even though a dollar's purchasing power never depends on its origin.
tell: You hold a "safe" index account and a "play" account that torches capital on options, while carrying credit card debt that outpaces both—three ledgers hiding one balance sheet.
counter: Consolidate everything into one net-worth view and judge risk at the household level.

name: Sunk Cost Fallacy
def: Resources already spent are unrecoverable, yet they keep steering choices: Arkes and Blumer (1985) showed people escalate commitment to a course simply because they have paid for it. Rational decisions weigh only future costs and benefits; sunk costs are relevant to accounting, not to choice.
tell: You keep the broken thesis because you have already spent forty hours modeling it, and you average down mostly to justify the hours, not the price.
counter: Ask what you would do starting fresh today; do that.

name: Endowment Effect
def: Kahneman, Knetsch, and Thaler demonstrated that merely owning something raises its perceived value: sellers demand roughly twice what buyers will pay for the identical item. Ownership converts a neutral asset into a possession, so parting with it registers as a loss.
tell: You would never buy your largest holding at today's price, yet selling it feels unthinkable—the position is defended not for its prospects but because it is yours.
counter: Apply the ownership test: if you would not buy it today, sell it.

name: Availability Heuristic
def: Tversky and Kahneman (1973) showed people judge probability by how easily examples come to mind, so vivid, recent, or heavily covered events feel more likely than they are. Frequency in memory substitutes for frequency in the world, and the news cycle sets the memory.
tell: One dramatic bankruptcy headline and you slash the whole sector's weighting, while the slow, unphotogenic risk—fee drag, concentration, inflation—never makes the worry list.
counter: Look up the base rate before reacting to any vivid headline.

name: Representativeness Heuristic
def: Kahneman and Tversky showed people judge probability by resemblance: how much a case looks like the stereotype of its category. Similarity crowds out base rates and sample size, so a company that feels like the next great compounder gets priced as one, whatever the odds.
tell: The pitch writes itself—founder-led, asset-light, "the next Amazon"—and your comps set skips the hundreds of lookalikes that quietly went nowhere.
counter: Price the base rate for the category first, the story second.

name: Gambler's Fallacy
def: In independent random processes, people expect deviations to self-correct: after five heads, tails feels "due." The belief comes from expecting small samples to look like long-run averages, but a fair coin has no memory and neither does a stock's daily return.
tell: Six red sessions in a row and you buy the dip purely because a bounce feels overdue—no valuation work, just the sense that the streak must break.
counter: Demand a fundamental reason for reversal; a streak is not one.

name: Hot Hand Fallacy
def: Gilovich, Vallone, and Tversky (1985) found basketball fans see streak shooters where their data showed none; people read skill and momentum into short runs of luck. In markets, a manager's three good years or a stock's winning streak gets treated as a persistent trait.
tell: You chase the fund topping the one-year leaderboard and add to whatever worked last month, paying up for a streak that statistics say is mostly noise.
counter: Judge track records over full cycles, never over the last hot stretch.

name: Home Bias
def: French and Poterba (1991) documented that investors overwhelmingly overweight their home country's equities, sacrificing diversification for familiarity. Local names feel safer because they are recognizable, yet familiarity is not information—your paycheck, housing, and portfolio end up betting on the same economy.
tell: Your portfolio is nearly all U.S. large caps plus your employer's stock, so one domestic downturn can hit your income and your savings in the same quarter.
counter: Set an explicit international target weight and rebalance to it on schedule.

name: Narrative Fallacy
def: The mind compresses messy, partly random events into tidy cause-and-effect stories—a tendency Taleb named the narrative fallacy. A good story feels like understanding, but the coherence is added after the fact, and the more satisfying the explanation, the less scrutiny it receives.
tell: Every close gets a clean explanation—"stocks fell on rate fears"—and your investment memos read like novels: compelling arcs, few disconfirmable numbers.
counter: Translate every story into numbers that could prove it wrong.

name: Survivorship Bias
def: Datasets and anecdotes quietly drop the failures, so the visible sample — funds still open, stocks still listed, founders still famous — overstates the odds of success. Inference drawn only from survivors flatters every backtest, every fund league table, and every strategy built on them.
tell: You screen for ten-year track records and conclude active managers beat the index, never counting the funds that closed or merged; your comp set in a model shows only companies healthy enough to still be trading.
counter: Ask what the full starting cohort looked like before trusting any sample of survivors.

name: Outcome Bias
def: A decision gets graded by how it turned out rather than by what was knowable when it was made, so lucky gambles read as skill and sound bets that missed read as blunders. Baron and Hershey documented the effect in 1988: identical choices, judged differently once results were revealed.
tell: A position doubles on a takeover rumor you never modeled and you file the trade as evidence your process works, while a well-researched thesis stopped out by a macro shock gets purged from the playbook.
counter: Grade the decision against the information you had at entry, not against the tape.

name: Self-Attribution Bias
def: Gains get credited to your own skill while losses get billed to bad luck, the market, or someone else's data, so feedback never lands where it should. Gervais and Odean showed the pattern breeds overconfidence: traders learn they are good faster than they learn they are wrong.
tell: Your journal reads "good call" on every winner and "Fed surprise" on every loser; after a hot quarter you size up, having attributed a rising tape to your own selection.
counter: Write the attribution for every loss before assigning credit for any win.

name: Illusion of Control
def: People behave as if skill can steer outcomes that are substantially chance, especially when the task offers familiar cues like choice, effort, and involvement. Langer showed in 1975 that merely picking one's own lottery ticket made people demand a higher price to sell it.
tell: You watch the tape all day and trade often because activity feels like control, or you trust a DCF to two decimals because you built every driver cell yourself.
counter: Separate what you can actually influence from what you can only forecast, and act on the first.

name: Planning Fallacy
def: Forecasts for your own projects cluster around the best case because the mind builds them from an inside view of the plan, not from how similar efforts actually finished. Kahneman and Tversky named the fallacy and prescribed the fix: start from the base rates of comparable projects.
tell: Your model assumes the synergies land in year one and the integration costs nothing extra, and your "quick" rebuild of the operating model takes three weekends instead of one.
counter: Anchor every timeline and synergy estimate on how comparable projects actually finished.

name: Base Rate Neglect
def: When a vivid specific story is available, the mind weights how well it fits the stereotype and ignores how often that outcome occurs in the population. Kahneman and Tversky showed judgments track representativeness while the prior probabilities — the base rates — get almost no weight.
tell: The pitch sounds exactly like the next great compounder, so you skip the boring question of what fraction of companies with this profile ever compound at all.
counter: Before pricing the story, look up how often stories like it come true.

name: Framing Effect
def: Logically identical choices produce different decisions depending on whether they are described as gains or losses, because preferences attach to the description, not the substance. Tversky and Kahneman demonstrated it in 1981: the same program saved lives or cost them depending only on wording.
tell: "90% of principal protected" gets your allocation while "10% of principal at risk" does not, and management's adjusted-EBITDA frame sets the anchor your whole comp sheet inherits.
counter: Restate every pitch in the opposite frame before deciding.

name: Ambiguity Aversion
def: People prefer known odds to unknown odds even when the expected payoff is the same, paying a premium to avoid situations where probabilities themselves are uncertain. Ellsberg's 1961 urn problems made the point: people consistently bet on the urn whose composition they knew.
tell: You crowd into familiar large caps and skip the misunderstood spinoff — not because the odds are worse, but because nobody has published the odds; the fuzzier the situation, the fatter the discount you demand.
counter: Price the uncertainty explicitly instead of refusing to bid on it.

name: Regret Aversion
def: Decisions get warped by the anticipated pain of future regret, so errors of commission feel costlier than errors of omission and action is avoided to keep blame avoidable. Shefrin and Statman put it at the heart of the disposition effect: selling a loser converts regret from paper to fact.
tell: You hold the loser because selling would make the mistake official, and you skip the obvious buy because a wrong action stings more than a wrong omission.
counter: Decide by expected value, then treat the regret as a cost you already paid.

name: Affect Heuristic
def: Judgments of risk and return ride on an instant feeling about the thing being judged: what feels good gets scored as high return and low risk together. Slovic and colleagues traced the pattern to affect: perceived benefit and perceived risk move inversely, though in markets they usually travel together.
tell: You love the product, so the stock reads safe and cheap at any multiple; a company you find dull gets a discount rate its actual cash flows never earned.
counter: Score risk and return separately, in numbers, before letting yourself like anything.

name: Conservatism Bias
def: New evidence gets underweighted relative to prior beliefs, so estimates update in the right direction but by too little and too slowly. Edwards documented it in the 1960s: given fresh data, people revised probabilities far less than Bayes' rule required.
tell: The company guides down hard and you trim your estimates five percent, keeping last quarter's thesis mostly intact; the model's growth ramp survives three straight earnings misses.
counter: When material news lands, rebuild the estimate from scratch rather than nudging the old one.

name: Halo Effect
def: One salient positive trait — a great brand, a charismatic CEO, a hot stock chart — spills over and inflates judgments of every unrelated attribute. Thorndike identified the pattern in 1920 when officers' ratings of soldiers on separate qualities correlated far too highly to be independent.
tell: An admired management team earns your model a lower discount rate, fatter margins, and a longer runway all at once — three separate assumptions moved by a single impression.
counter: Rate each assumption on its own evidence, blind to your overall impression.

name: Authority Bias
def: Statements gain unearned credibility from the status of the speaker, so titles, banks, and famous track records substitute for checking the argument itself. Milgram's obedience experiments showed how far compliance stretches when an authority takes responsibility for the outcome.
tell: A bulge-bracket price target overrides your own math, and you copy a famous investor's 13F position without knowing the cost basis, hedges, or reasoning — only the name.
counter: Strip the name off the argument and see if the numbers still persuade you.

name: Ostrich Effect
def: Investors selectively avoid information that threatens to hurt, checking portfolios eagerly in rising markets and going quiet when prices fall. Karlsson, Loewenstein, and Seppi documented the pattern in account-login data: attention itself gets managed to protect current mood.
tell: You haven't opened the brokerage app since the drawdown started, and the losing position's tab in your tracking sheet quietly stops getting updated.
counter: Review the portfolio on a fixed calendar, especially in weeks you dread it.

name: Naive Diversification
def: Offered a menu of options, people spread money evenly across whatever is listed rather than weighting by risk, so the menu design dictates the allocation. Benartzi and Thaler called it the 1/n heuristic after finding 401(k) allocations tracked the number of funds offered.
tell: You own ten positions at ten percent each and call it diversified, though four are the same semiconductor bet and your risk sits wherever the tickers happened to cluster.
counter: Weight by correlated exposure, not by line count in the account.

name: Money Illusion
def: The mind reasons in nominal dollars, treating a number on a check or a screen as value while inflation quietly rewrites what it buys. Shafir, Diamond, and Tversky showed the nominal frame shapes judgments of fairness, happiness, and even whether a raise counts as a raise.
tell: You celebrate a 5% portfolio return in a 4% inflation year as a win, and your model discounts real cash flows at a nominal rate without noticing the mismatch.
counter: State every return, raise, and hurdle in real terms before judging it.

name: Present Bias
def: Rewards available now get weighted far beyond what consistent discounting allows, so the trade-off between today and tomorrow keeps flipping in today's favor. Laibson formalized it as quasi-hyperbolic discounting: patient in every plan, impatient at every moment of execution.
tell: The IRA contribution keeps losing to this month's spending, and you book the small gain today rather than let a working thesis compound for another year.
counter: Automate the contribution so the future self never has to outvote the present one.

name: Winner's Curse
def: In any auction where the asset's value is roughly common to all bidders, the winner is systematically the bidder who overestimated it most. The pattern was first documented by petroleum engineers studying oil-lease auctions, and Thaler made it a staple of behavioral economics.
tell: Your LBO model "wins" the deal only after the third round of raising the exit multiple, and the stock you finally got filled on is the one everyone else stopped bidding for.
counter: Set your walk-away price before the bidding starts and shade it down when rivals are many.

name: Myopic Loss Aversion
def: Loss aversion combined with frequent portfolio checking makes a fundamentally attractive gamble look bad, because short evaluation windows show losses roughly half the time. Benartzi and Thaler used the combination to explain the equity premium puzzle: investors who evaluate annually demand outsized compensation for holding stocks.
tell: You check the account daily, feel every red day twice as hard as every green one, and end up trimming equities a ten-year plan says you should hold.
counter: Match how often you look at the portfolio to the horizon of the money in it.

name: Attention Bias
def: Individual investors buy from the narrow set of stocks that grabbed their attention — news, volume spikes, extreme returns — because searching thousands of names is otherwise impossible. Barber and Odean showed attention drives buying far more than selling, and the resulting purchases earn no edge.
tell: Every ticker in your book arrived through a headline, a screener spike, or a feed — your watchlist is whatever shouted loudest, not whatever screened cheapest.
counter: Source ideas from a systematic screen you run on schedule, not from the loudest headlines.
